questions.prompt: Complete: "She ___ start a new job next month." (a plan already decided)
questions.explanation: With she, be going to is is going to + base verb: She is going to start a new job. "are going to" is for you/we/they, "going to" alone drops the be, and "is go to" mangles the verb form.

questions.prompt: Complete: "The bag is heavy. I ___ carry it for you." (you decide right now)
questions.explanation: An offer decided as you speak uses will (short form 'll) + base verb: I'll carry it. "'ll to carry" wrongly adds to after will, "carry" alone is the present, and "am carry" is not a real form.

questions.prompt: Complete: "They ___ buy a new car. They've saved enough money."
questions.explanation: With they, be going to is are going to: They are going to buy a new car. "is going to" is singular (he/she/it), "going to" drops the be (are), and "will to" is never correct — will takes no to.

questions.prompt: Complete the prediction: "I think it ___ be sunny tomorrow." (just my opinion)
questions.explanation: A prediction based on opinion or belief ("I think…") uses will + base verb: it will be sunny. "will to" adds an extra to, while "is going" and "going to" are both broken going-to forms here.

questions.prompt: Complete the negative: "We ___ go to the beach; the weather is too bad."
questions.explanation: The negative of going to puts not on be: We aren't going to go. "isn't going to" is singular (he/she/it), "don't going to" uses the wrong helper, and "are going to" is affirmative, not negative.

questions.prompt: Complete: "A: I forgot my pen. B: No problem, I ___ lend you one."
questions.explanation: B offers help on the spot, deciding as they speak, so use will + base verb: I'll lend you one. "am going to" would imply a pre-made plan, "going to" alone is incomplete, and "will to" adds an incorrect to.
